Slide Bullet Point: Welcome to Electrical Fundamentals for ELV Engineers.
Core focus on basic electrical parameters and circuits.
Roadmap of the 12 slides in this session.
Matter consists of atoms containing protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Free electrons in copper move easily, creating electrical current.
Insulating materials (rubber, PVC) block electron flow.
Voltage (V) is the electromotive force driving charge carriers.
Current (I) is the rate of charge flow measured in Amperes.
Ohm's Law states: Current = Voltage / Resistance (I = V/R).
Resistance (R) opposes current flow, converting energy to heat.
Joule heating formula (P = IÂ²R) explains power dissipation.
Selecting correct resistor wattage prevents thermal failure.
Electrical power (P) is the rate of energy consumption in Watts.
Watt's Law states: Power = Voltage Ã— Current (P = V Ã— I).
Sizing power supplies requires calculating total system wattage.
Alternating Current (AC) periodically reverses direction.
Direct Current (DC) flows continuously in a single direction.
DC ripple represents residual AC voltage on a rectified DC line.
Carbon film, metal film, and wirewound resistor profiles.
Read four-band and five-band color codes to find ohm values.
Understanding tolerance ratings (gold = 5%, silver = 10%).
Series connection adds resistances: R_total = R1 + R2 + R3.
Parallel connection reduces total resistance: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2.
Applications in access control loop resistor circuits.
In series circuits, current is constant; voltage splits.
In parallel circuits, voltage is constant; current splits.
Most ELV hardware and cameras are wired in parallel configurations.
Staircase wiring uses two single-pole double-throw (SPDT) switches.
Allows controlling a single light source from two separate locations.
Phase/Line terminal routing standard for staircase safety.
Stripping wire insulation without damaging internal copper cores.
Using bootlace ferrule terminals to prevent wire fraying inside blocks.
Selecting the right crimping tool profiles for different wire gauges.
Wire up staircase circuits on a testboard.
Measure series and parallel voltages using a digital multimeter.
Crimp bootlace ferrules onto multi-strand power cables.
Welcome to Earthing & Lightning Protection.
Focus on system safety, ground paths, and surge suppression.
Overview of the 10 slides in this session.
Earthing provides a low-resistance path for fault currents to return to ground.
Protects users from electric shock due to insulation failure.
Stabilizes voltage levels during transient faults.
Understanding TN-S, TN-C, and TT grounding setups.
Local copper plate, rod, and chemical gel earthing options.
Soil moisture enhancement treatments for dry regions.
Ground resistivity depends on soil type, moisture, and salt content.
Chemical compounds (bentonite, charcoal) lower resistance.
Performing soil resistivity tests before drilling earth pits.
Safe earth resistance should ideally be under 5 Ohms.
High-sensitivity instrumentation systems require under 1 Ohm.
Testing resistance periodically using earth tester meters.
Franklin rods, early streamer emission (ESE), and Faraday cages.
Down conductors path routing guidelines.
Maintaining physical separation from electrical wires.
Metal Oxide Varistors (MOVs) clamp transient voltage surges.
Gas Discharge Tubes (GDTs) handle high-current lightning discharges.
Sizing SPDs based on maximum continuous operating voltage (MCOV).
Mounting Type 1 and Type 2 SPDs inside distribution boards.
Keeping connection lead lengths under 50cm to minimize impedance.
Wiring Earth lines directly to the main grounding busbar.
Preventing ground loop noise in analog CCTV networks.
Shield grounding protocols for STP Ethernet and RS485 communication cables.
Isolated grounding bars for sensitive server racks.
Measure earth pit resistance using a three-point fall-of-potential tester.
Inspect chemical earthing rods and compound treatments.
Install and wire a surge protection module in a test DB panel.
